turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan has our opinion this morning in case 16-32, Kindred Nursing Centers versus Clark.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: When parties sign an arbitration agreement, they agree not to go to court to resolve their disputes, but instead to use an alternative system of dispute resolution involving private arbitrators. Among other things, that means a person with a complaint against someone else waives their right to a trial by jury. For many years Courts, especially state courts viewed arbitration agreements with great suspicion and tried hard not to enforce them. As a result, Congress which generally liked the arbitration systems passed the Federal Arbitration Act or FAA. It requires all courts to treat arbitration agreements in the same way as other contracts. That means a court may strike down an arbitration agreement for reasons that would apply to contracts generally. But a court may not adopt rules that single out those agreements for disfavored or especially bad treatment. Now in this case, Beverly Wellner and Janis Clark, each signed an arbitration agreement with Kindred Nursing Centers on behalf of a family member who had moved in to a Kindred Nursing Home. One family member was a husband, the other a mother. Later, those family members died and Beverly and Janis sued Kindred on behalf of their estates for delivering substandard care. Kindred responded by asking the Kentucky courts to enforce the arbitration agreements and so dismiss the lawsuits. Instead, the Kentucky Supreme Court held that the arbitration agreements were invalid. Janis and Beverly's authority to sign those agreements on behalf of their family members came from legal documents known as “powers of attorney”. And according to the state court, a power of attorney cannot authorize you to sign an arbitration agreement for someone else unless it says in crystal clear and specific terms that you may waive that person's right to a jury trial. Under that rule, a general authorization, for example, one saying that you can sign all contracts on another person's behalf is not enough. The question in this case is whether that clear statement rule complies with the FAA's equal treatment principle? We hold that it does not. The court's rule was based on the importance of the jury trial right under the state constitution. But as I just explained, waiving the jury trial right is what arbitration agreements do, pretty much by definition. So, applying an especially onerous rule to agreements that waive jury trial rights is really just another way of discriminating against or disfavoring arbitration agreements, and that is exactly what Congress enacting the FAA said the courts could not do. We reverse the Kentucky Supreme Court's opinion in part and vacate it in part to allow the courts to consider an alternative basis for invalidating one of the arbitration agreements. Justice Thomas has filed a dissenting opinion. Justice Gorsuch took no part in the consideration or decision of this case.
